item.title: 【A mid-year 2025 look at CNCF, Linux Foundation, and the top 30 open source projects】2025 年中 CNCF、Linux 基金会和前 30 个开源项目一览
item.description:
【

Building upon our previous analyses, we continue to monitor trends and technologies that resonate with developers and end users. Take a look at our past timeframes from our blogs. 

Here are the main takeaways I see from the charts.

  • Kubernetes maintains its position as the project with the largest contributor base, reflecting its continued maturity and widespread adoption.
  • OpenTelemetry continues to grow its contributor base and remains the second highest velocity project in CNCF and has become the “kubernetes” of the o11y world.
  • Backstage addresses developer experience pain points and has become the most popular open source IdP in the world.
  • GitOps remains integral to the cloud native ecosystem, with projects like Argo and Flux cultivating large communities.
  • Crossplane has seen a contributor base growth of over 20% in the last year, highlighting the demand for open source control planes and multi cloud management.
  • Kubeflow has entered the top 30 CNCF project list, underscoring the role of CNCF projects in underpinning large-scale AI infrastructure.

NOTE: We use bubble charts to show three axes of data: commits, authors, and comments/pull requests, and plot on a log-log chart to show the data across large scales. 

  • The bubble’s area is proportional to the number of authors 
  • The y-axis is the total number of pull requests and issues 
  • The x-axis is the number of commits

All of the current and past reports are available on GitHub.

All of the scripts used to generate this data are at https://github.com/cncf/velocity (under an Apache 2.0 license). If you see any errors, please open an issue there.

Past blog posts about project velocity:

  • 2024 year in review of CNCF and top 30 open source project velocity
  • wasmCloud on the factory floor: efficient and secure processing of high velocity machine data
  • As we reach mid-year 2024, a look at CNCF, Linux Foundation, and top 30 open source project velocity 
  • A look back at CNCF, Linux Foundation, and top 30 open source project velocity in 2023 
  • October 2023: where we are with velocity of CNCF, LF, and top 30 open source projects
  • A look at the 2022 velocity of CNCF, Linux Foundation, and top 30 open source projects
  • Mid-year update on 2022 CNCF, Linux Foundation, and open source velocity
  • End of year update on CNCF and open source velocity in 2021
  • Update on CNCF and Open Source Project Velocity 2020
  • The 30 highest velocity open source projects 

】

基于我们之前的分析，我们继续监控与开发者和最终用户产生共鸣的趋势和技术。从我们的博客查看我们过去的时间范围。 

以下是我从图表中看到的主要内容。

  • Kubernetes 保持了其作为贡献者基数最大的项目的地位，反映出其持续成熟和广泛采用。
  • OpenTelemetry 不断扩大其贡献者基础，仍然是 CNCF 中第二高速度的项目，并已成为 o11y 世界的“kubernetes”。
  • Backstage 解决了开发者体验痛点，并已成为世界上最受欢迎的开源 IdP。
  • GitOps 仍然是云原生生态系统不可或缺的一部分，Argo 和 Flux 等项目培育了大型社区。
  • Crossplane 去年的贡献者基数增长了 20% 以上，凸显了对开源控制平面和多云管理的需求。
  • Kubeflow 已进入 CNCF 项目前 30 名，凸显了 CNCF 项目在支撑大规模人工智能基础设施方面的作用。

注意：我们使用气泡图来显示数据的三个轴：提交、作者和评论/拉取请求，并绘制在对数图上以显示大范围的数据。 

  • 气泡面积与作者数量成正比
  • y 轴是拉取请求和问题的总数
  • x 轴是提交次数

所有当前和过去报告可在 GitHub 上获取。

用于生成此数据的所有脚本均位于 https://github.com/cncf/velocity（采用 Apache 2.0 许可证）。如果您发现任何错误，请在那里提出问题。

过去有关项目速度的博客文章：

  • 2024 年 CNCF 回顾和前 30 名开源项目速度
  • 工厂车间的 wasmCloud：高效、安全地处理高速机器数据
  • 到了 2024 年年中，我们来看看 CNCF、Linux 基金会和排名前 30 的开源项目速度
  • 回顾 CNCF、Linux 基金会和 2023 年排名前 30 的开源项目速度
  • 2023 年 10 月：CNCF、LF 和前 30 个开源项目的速度
  • CNCF、Linux 基金会和前 30 个开源项目 2022 年速度一览
  • 2022 年 CNCF、Linux 基金会和开源速度的年中更新
  • 2021 年 CNCF 和开源速度年终更新
  • CNCF 和开源项目 Velocity 2020 更新
  • 速度最快的 30 个开源项目

item.title: 【A guide to choosing an Ingress Controller, part 1: identify your requirements】Ingress 控制器选择指南，第 1 部分：确定您的要求
item.description:
【

Guest post originally published on the NGINX blog by Jenn Gile, Manager, Product Marketing at F5

This is the first blog post in our series on how to choose a Kubernetes Ingress controller.

  • A Guide to Choosing an Ingress Controller, Part 1: Identify Your Requirements (this post)
  • A Guide to Choosing an Ingress Controller, Part 2: Risks and Future-Proofing
  • A Guide to Choosing an Ingress Controller, Part 3: Open Source vs. Default vs. Commercial
  • A Guide to Choosing an Ingress Controller, Part 4: NGINX Ingress Controller Options

When organizations first start experimenting with Kubernetes, they often don’t put a lot of thought into the choice of Ingress controller. They might think that all Ingress controllers are alike, and in the interests of getting up and running quickly it’s easiest to stick with the default Ingress controller for the Kubernetes framework they did choose. And it’s true that just about any Ingress controller is fine in testing or low‑volume production environments. But as you scale, your choice of Ingress controller becomes more important. That’s because Ingress controllers can provide much more than the basic functionality of moving your traffic from point A to point B.

From advanced traffic management to visibility to built‑in security, an Ingress controller can be one of the most powerful tools in your Kubernetes stack. In fact, at F5 NGINX we consider it to be the foundation of any production‑grade Kubernetes deployment. But many developers and Platform Ops teams don’t realize the full power of an Ingress controller – or the consequences of choosing one that can’t scale. Choosing an Ingress controller that doesn’t scale well or protect complex environments can prevent you from getting Kubernetes out of testing and into production. In this blog series we aim to educate you on the basics of Ingress controllers and how to make a wise choice that delivers the functionality and security you need, today and tomorrow.

What’s an Ingress Controller?

An Ingress controller is a specialized load balancer that manages the Layer 4 and 7 traffic entering Kubernetes clusters, and potentially the traffic exiting them. So that we’re all on the same page, here are the terms we use at NGINX (and they’re largely the same across the industry):

  • Ingress traffic – Traffic entering a Kubernetes cluster
  • Egress traffic – Traffic exiting a Kubernetes cluster
  • North‑south traffic – Traffic entering and exiting a Kubernetes cluster (also called ingress‑egress traffic)
  • East‑west traffic – Traffic moving among services within a Kubernetes cluster (also called service-to-service traffic)
  • Service mesh – A traffic management tool for routing and securing service-to-service traffic

Why Do You Need an Ingress Controller?

By default, applications running in Kubernetes pods (containers) are not accessible from the external network, but only from other pods within the Kubernetes cluster. Kubernetes has a built‑in configuration object for HTTP load balancing, called Ingress, which defines how entities outside a Kubernetes cluster can connect to the pods represented by one or more Kubernetes services.

When you need to provide external access to your Kubernetes services, you create an Ingress resource to define the connectivity rules, including the URI path, backing service name, and other information. On its own, however, the Ingress resource doesn’t do anything. You must deploy and configure an Ingress controller application (using the Kubernetes API) to implement the rules defined in Ingress resources.

What Does an Ingress Controller Do?

  • Accepts traffic from outside the Kubernetes environment, potentially modify (shape) it, and distribute it to pods running inside the environment. The Ingress controller replaces the default kube-proxy traffic distribution model, giving you additional controls like those that application delivery controllers (ADCs) and reverse proxies provide in non‑Kubernetes environments.
  • Monitors the individual pods of a service, guaranteeing intelligent routing and preventing requests from being “black‑holed“.
  • Implements egress rules to enhance security with mutual TLS (mTLS) or limit outgoing traffic from certain pods to specific external services.

When it’s time to select an Ingress controller, it can be tempting to start with a feature list, but you might end up with an Ingress controller that has fantastic features but doesn’t satisfy your business needs. Instead make sure to explore two elements that impact how well the Ingress controller will work for your team and your apps: use cases (what problems it will solve) and resourcing (how you’re going to “pay” for it). We’ll cover these two topics in the remainder of this blog.

What Problems Do You Want the Ingress Controller to Solve?

The core use case for an Ingress controller is traffic management, so you probably want the Ingress controller to handle one or more of these common use cases:

  • Load balancing (HTTP2, HTTP/HTTPS, SSL/TLS termination, TCP/UDP, WebSocket, gRPC)
  • Traffic control (rate limiting, circuit breaking, active health checks)
  • Traffic splitting (debug routing, A/B testing, canary deployments, blue‑green deployments)

But there’s no reason to settle for a “one‑trick pony” – most Ingress controllers can do more than manage traffic. By using the Ingress controller to solve multiple problems, not only do you reduce the size and complexity of your stack, but you can also offload non‑functional requirements from the apps to the Ingress controller. Let’s look at four non‑traditional Ingress controller use cases that can help make your Kubernetes deployments more secure, agile, and scalable while making more efficient use of your resources.

Monitoring and Visibility

Lack of visibility into the Kubernetes cluster is one of the biggest challenges in production environments, contributing to difficulty with troubleshooting and resiliency. Because Ingress controllers operate at the edge of your Kubernetes clusters and touch every bit of traffic, they’re well situated to provide data that can help you troubleshoot (and even avoid) two common problems: slow apps and resource exhaustion in the Kubernetes cluster or platform. For Ingress controller to improve visibility, it needs to:

  • Provide metrics in real time so you can diagnose what’s happening “right now”
  • Be able to export metrics to popular visibility tools, like Prometheus and Grafana, that plot values over time to help you predict traffic surges and other trends

API Gateway

Unless you’re looking to perform request‑response manipulation in Kubernetes, it’s very likely that the Ingress controller can double as your API gateway. Depending on its feature set, an Ingress controller might be able to provide core API gateway functions including TLS termination, client authentication, rate limiting, fine‑grained access control, and request routing at Layers 4 through 7.

Authentication and Single Sign‑On

Offloading authentication of login credentials from your Kubernetes services to your Ingress controller can solve two issues.

  • Enable users to log into multiple apps with a single set of credentials by implementing single‑sign on (SSO) with OpenID Connect (OIDC).
  • Eliminate the need to build authentication functionality into each application, allowing your developers to focus on the business logic of their apps.

Web Application Firewall Integration

It’s not that an Ingress controller can serve as a web application firewall (WAF), but rather that the WAF can be consolidated with the Ingress controller. Although a WAF can be deployed at many places outside and within Kubernetes, for most organizations the most efficient and effective location is in the same pod as the Ingress controller. This use case is perfect when security policies are under the direction of SecOps or DevSecOps and when a fine‑grained, per‑service or per‑URI approach is needed. It means you can use the Kubernetes API to define policies and associate them with services. Further, the Ingress controller’s role‑based access control (RBAC) functionality can enable SecOps to enforce policies per listener while DevOps users set policies per Ingress resource.

How Are You Going to Resource the Ingress Controller?

Every Ingress controller comes at a cost…even those that free and open source (perhaps you’ve heard the phrase “free like a puppy”). Some costs can be assigned predictable dollar amounts as line items in your budget, while others depend on how much time your team has to spend dealing with the consequences of which Ingress controller you choose (increased complexity, lack of portability, and so on). Let’s look at the costs – in terms of both time and money – to consider when budgeting for an Ingress controller: time and money.

Budgeting for Time Costs

Budgeting for headcount can be far more challenging than for fixed‑cost line items, especially when you’re trying to resource a project in an unfamiliar space. You need to ask questions like:

  • Who will configure and administer the Ingress controller? Is it part of their full‑time job (for example, as members of your Platform Ops team) or are they taking it on as an extra responsibility (as developers)?
  • Can you make time for employees to take formal training? Or must the tool be simple enough to learn quickly and easily on the job?
  • Are you prepared to have employees tinker with it whenever a new feature is needed, or spend extensive time troubleshooting when there’s a problem? Or do you need them to deliver other business value?

A Note on Kubernetes Tool Ownership

We’ve observed a trend in the industry toward consolidating tools and ownership under the domain of NetOps teams. While this can go a long way towards simplifying your stack and improving security, we advocate for thoughtful duplication of tools based on team goals. It makes sense to have NetOps team manage perimeter tools (like external load balancers) because they focus on the broader platform, but DevOps teams care most about the services deployed close to the app code and need more agility and finer‑grained control than they get from NetOps tools. Kubernetes tools, including the Ingress controller, have the best chance of success when they’re selected by DevOps. That’s not to say you must grant developers complete freedom of choice for tools! Some brutal standardization of tooling within Kubernetes is still a best practice.

Budgeting for Capital Costs

When organizations first experiment with Kubernetes, they often don’t budget much for tools or support. If you have the people resources to truly support an Ingress controller that needs more “hand holding”, then no monetary budget is okay…at first. But as your Kubernetes investment increases, you may find yourself limited by the tool’s features, or wanting to dedicate your team to other priorities. That’s when the scale tips towards paying for an easier-to-use, more stable tool with enterprise features and support.

When you’re ready to pay for an Ingress controller, be aware that the licensing model matters. Based on traditional pricing models outside of Kubernetes, pricing for Ingress controllers is often “per instance” or “per Ingress proxy”. While there are use cases when this still makes sense in Kubernetes, licensing an Ingress controller on a “per cluster” basis instead means you pay based on application tenancy rather than “number of proxies.”

Here are our recommendations for different scenarios:

  • New to Kubernetes? Choose per‑cluster licensing.
    When you’re inexperienced with Kubernetes, it’s very difficult to accurately predict the number of Ingress controller instances you need. If forced to choose a number of instances, you may underestimate – making it difficult to achieve your goals – or overestimate and waste dollars better spent on other parts of the Kubernetes project. Negotiating a relatively low fixed price for a “small cluster” increases your chance for success.
  • Scaling Kubernetes? Choose per‑cluster licensing.
    It’s nearly impossible to predict how much and how often you’ll need to scale Kubernetes resources up and down (bursting and collapsing). Per‑instance pricing forces your team to impose arbitrary thresholds to stay within licensing caps. With per‑cluster licensing, you don’t have to track individual Ingress containers, and depending on the vendor (such as NGINX), bursting may be included at no additional cost.
  • Advanced or static deployments? Choose per‑instance licensing.
    If you’re savvy enough with Kubernetes to know exactly how you’re going to use the Ingress controller, and you plan to use a small number of Ingress proxies per‑cluster, and you don’t need any bundled services that might come along with the tool, then per‑instance pricing can be a great choice.

Next Steps: Risk Tolerance and Future‑Proofing

Now that you have a grasp on your requirements, the next step is to decide as a team what your tolerance is for the risks an Ingress controller might introduce, and to figure out how you’ll need the Ingress controller to scale as you grow your Kubernetes deployment. We take up those topics in Part 2.

】

客座帖子最初由 F5 产品营销经理 Jenn Gile 在NGINX 博客上发布

这是我们关于如何选择 Kubernetes Ingress 控制器的系列文章中的第一篇博文。

  • 入口控制器选择指南，第 1 部分：确定您的要求（本文）
  • 入口控制器选择指南，第 2 部分：风险和面向未来
  • 入口控制器选择指南，第 3 部分：开源、默认与商业
  • 入口控制器选择指南选择入口控制器，第 4 部分：NGINX 入口控制器选项

当组织第一次开始尝试 Kubernetes 时，他们通常不会对入口控制器的选择进行太多思考。他们可能认为所有 Ingress 控制器都是相似的，为了快速启动和运行，最简单的方法是坚持使用他们选择的 Kubernetes 框架的默认 Ingress 控制器。确实，几乎任何 Ingress 控制器都适合测试或小批量生产环境。但随着规模的扩大，您对 Ingress 控制器的选择变得更加重要。这是因为 Ingress 控制器可以提供的不仅仅是将流量从 A 点移动到 B 点的基本功能。

从高级流量管理到可见性再到内置安全性，Ingress 控制器可以成为 Kubernetes 堆栈中最强大的工具之一。事实上，在 F5 NGINX，我们认为它是任何生产级 Kubernetes 部署的基础。但许多开发者和平台运营团队并没有意识到 Ingress 控制器的全部功能，也没有意识到选择无法扩展的控制器的后果。选择不能很好扩展或不能保护复杂环境的 Ingress 控制器可能会阻止您将 Kubernetes 从测试中转移到生产中。在本博客系列中，我们旨在向您介绍 Ingress 控制器的基础知识，以及如何做出明智的选择，以提供您当前和未来所需的功能和安全性。

什么是入口控制器？

Ingress 控制器是一种专门的负载均衡器，用于管理进入 Kubernetes 集群的第 4 层和第 7 层流量，以及可能离开集群的流量。为了让我们达成共识，以下是我们在 NGINX 中使用的术语（它们在整个行业中基本相同）：

  • 入口流量 – 进入 Kubernetes 集群的流量
  • 出口流量 – 离开 Kubernetes 集群的流量
  • 南北向流量 – 进出 Kubernetes 集群的流量（也称为入口-出口流量）
  • 东西向流量流量 – Kubernetes 集群内的服务之间移动的流量（也称为服务到服务流量）
  • 服务网格 – 用于路由和保护服务到服务流量的流量管理工具

为什么需要入口控制器？

默认情况下，无法从外部网络访问 Kubernetes Pod（容器）中运行的应用程序，只能从 Kubernetes 集群内的其他 Pod 访问。 Kubernetes 有一个用于 HTTP 负载平衡的内置配置对象，称为 Ingress，它定义 Kubernetes 集群外部的实体如何连接到由一个或多个 Kubernetes 服务表示的 Pod。

当您需要提供对 Kubernetes 服务的外部访问时，您可以创建Ingress 资源来定义连接规则，包括 URI 路径、支持服务名称和其他信息。然而，Ingress 资源本身不执行任何操作。您必须部署和配置 Ingress 控制器应用程序（使用 Kubernetes API）来实现 Ingress 资源中定义的规则。

入口控制器有什么作用？

  • 接受来自 Kubernetes 环境外部的流量，可能对其进行修改（调整），并将其分发到环境内运行的 Pod。 Ingress 控制器取代了默认的kube-proxy 流量分配模型，为您提供了额外的控制功能，例如应用程序交付控制器 (ADC) 和反向代理在非 Kubernetes 环境中提供的控制功能。
  • 监控某个容器的各个 Pod。服务，保证智能路由并防止请求成为“黑洞”。
  • 实施出口规则以通过双向 TLS (mTLS) 增强安全性或限制从某些 Pod 到特定外部服务的传出流量。

当需要选择 Ingress 控制器时，您可能会很想从功能列表开始，但您最终可能会得到一个具有出色功能但无法满足您的业务需求的 Ingress 控制器。相反，请确保探索影响 Ingress 控制器为您的团队和应用程序工作的两个要素：用例（它将解决什么问题）和资源（您将如何为其“付费”）。我们将在本博客的其余部分讨论这两个主题。

您希望 Ingress Controller 解决什么问题？

Ingress 控制器的核心用例是流量管理，因此您可能希望 Ingress 控制器处理以下一个或多个常见用例：

  • 负载均衡（HTTP2、HTTP/HTTPS、SSL/TLS 终止、TCP/UDP、WebSocket、gRPC）
  • 流量控制（速率限制、熔断、主动健康检查）
  • 流量拆分（调试路由、A/B 测试、金丝雀部署、蓝绿部署）

但是没有理由满足于“一招小马”——大多数 Ingress 控制器不仅仅可以管理流量。通过使用 Ingress 控制器解决多个问题，您不仅可以减小堆栈的大小和复杂性，还可以将非功能性需求从应用程序卸载到 Ingress 控制器。让我们看一下四个非传统 Ingress 控制器用例，它们可以帮助您的 Kubernetes 部署更加安全、敏捷和可扩展，同时更有效地利用您的资源。

监控和可见性

缺乏对 Kubernetes 集群的可见性是生产环境中最大的挑战之一，导致故障排除和恢复能力变得困难。由于 Ingress 控制器在 Kubernetes 集群的边缘运行并触及每一点流量，因此它们能够很好地提供数据，帮助您解决（甚至避免）两个常见问题：应用程序速度缓慢以及 Kubernetes 集群或平台中的资源耗尽。为了提高 Ingress 控制器的可见性，它需要：

  • 实时提供指标，以便您可以诊断“现在”发生的情况
  • 能够将指标导出到流行的可视性工具（例如 Prometheus 和 Grafana），这些工具可以随时间绘制值，以帮助您预测流量激增和其他趋势

API 网关

除非您希望在 Kubernetes 中执行请求响应操作，否则 Ingress 控制器很可能可以兼作您的 API 网关。根据其功能集，Ingress 控制器可能能够提供核心 API 网关功能，包括 TLS 终止、客户端身份验证、速率限制、细粒度访问控制以及第 4 层到第 7 层的请求路由。

身份验证和单点登录

将登录凭据的身份验证从 Kubernetes 服务卸载到 Ingress 控制器可以解决两个问题。

  • 通过 OpenID Connect (OIDC) 实施单点登录 (SSO)，让用户能够使用一组凭据登录多个应用。
  • 无需在每个应用中构建身份验证功能，让开发人员能够专注于其应用的业务逻辑。

Web 应用程序防火墙集成

并不是说 Ingress 控制器可以充当 Web 应用防火墙 (WAF)，而是 WAF 可以与 Ingress 控制器整合。虽然WAF 可以部署在 Kubernetes 外部和内部的许多地方，对于大多数组织来说，最高效和有效的位置是与 Ingress 控制器位于同一个 pod 中。当安全策略处于 SecOps 或 DevSecOps 的指导下，并且需要细粒度、按服务或按 URI 的方法时，此用例是完美的。这意味着您可以使用 Kubernetes API 来定义策略并将其与服务关联。此外，Ingress 控制器的基于角色的访问控制 (RBAC) 功能可以使 SecOps 为每个侦听器强制执行策略，同时 DevOps 用户为每个 Ingress 资源设置策略。

您将如何为 Ingress 控制器提供资源？

每个 Ingress 控制器都是有成本的……即使是那些免费和开源的控制器（也许您听说过“像小狗一样自由”这句话）。有些成本可以作为预算中的行项目分配可预测的金额，而其他成本则取决于您的团队需要花费多少时间来处理您选择的 Ingress 控制器的后果（复杂性增加、缺乏可移植性等）。让我们看看在为 Ingress 控制器制定预算时要考虑的成本（时间和金钱）：时间和金钱。

item.title: 【Phippy and friends: bringing cloud native concepts to the masses】Phippy 和朋友们：将云原生概念带给大众
item.description:
【

At KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America last week, we welcomed Hazel the Hedgehog and Linky the Lobster to the Phippy and Friends family, thanks to the Helm and Linkerd project teams. Hazel and Linky not only represent the hard work and imagination of the maintainers, they help illustrate the concept of Helm Charts and Service Mesh. Next time you’re breaking down these concepts for folx who don’t share your background knowledge, how about using Linky and Hazel? In fact, you can even borrow pages from the Cartografos Group’s new book: Admiral Bash’s Island Adventure with Phippy and Friends. 

Are you a maintainer on a graduated project? Want to donate a character? Learn more here and fashion a Phippy!

Jumping through Jargon

Cloud Native computing is an incredible space, but it’s often tricky to explain in ways that non technical folx can grasp. Even experienced engineers find it challenging sometimes. The CNCF exists not just to advance the cloud native ecosystem, but to help folx from all backgrounds embrace technology, get excited about the possibilities it offers, and to ultimately join #TeamCloudNative. 

We know that tech is becoming more and more diverse — which is fantastic —  but we want to keep encouraging more folx from all walks of life to consider a cloud native career. After all, there is so much evidence that shows how diversity, equity and inclusivity (DEI) in open source impacts positively on society as a whole. A BCG study of more than 1,700 companies around the world revealed that diversity boosts innovation by expanding the range of a company’s ideas and options, leading to better financial performance. Together we are stronger, faster, better, and more innovative.

One way we can continue to boost DEI is to improve our technical communications. When we talk about tech in an accessible, straight-forward manner, we open the doors to more folx than ever before — folx who might think that tech is far too complicated.

In come Phippy and Friends

In 2016, Deis (now part of Microsoft Azure) platform architect Matt Butcher was looking for a way to explain Kubernetes to technical and non-technical people alike. Inspired by his daughter’s prolific stuffed animal collection, he came up with the idea of “The Children’s Illustrated Guide to Kubernetes.” Together with Karen Chu, and Bailey Beougher, he created Phippy, the yellow PHP application giraffe, and set her off on her first adventure.

In 2018, Phippy was donated to the Linux Foundation and today, the Phippy characters are free to use to teach others about cloud native computing. 

Phippy’s mission

From a humble PHP app, Phippy has gone on to help thousands of folx take their first steps to understanding cloud native computing — from containerisation to automation. Today, Phippy and Friends’ mission is to demystify cloud native computing and explain complicated concepts in a compelling, engaging and easy-to-understand manner, through books, presentations and even Twitter threads.

It’s not just for bedtime stories, you’re free to use the Phippy and Friends characters in your day-to-day work, especially when you’re tasked with explaining your team’s projects and products to folx who don’t share the same technical background. 

Phippy Collateral 

The Phippy and friends characters (Phippy, Goldie, Captain Kube, Zee, Hazel and Linky) are licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution License (CC-BY), which means that you can remix, transform, and build upon the material for any purpose, even commercially. If you use the characters, please include the text “phippy.io” to provide attribution (and online, please include a link to https://phippy.io).

You can download images of the Phippy characters in the CNCF artwork repo, in svg, png, and ai formats, in color, black, and white.

Don’t forget — there is also Phippy and Friends swag available, including hard-copies of Phippy’s books, including the brand-new book Admiral Bash’s Island Adventure, and plush toys, on the CNCF store. 

】

上周在北美 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 大会上，感谢 Helm 和 Linkerd 项目团队，我们迎来了 Hazel the Hedgehog 和 Linky the Lobster 加入 Phippy and Friends 大家庭。 Hazel 和 Linky 不仅代表了维护人员的辛勤工作和想象力，它们还有助于阐释 Helm Charts 和 Service Mesh 的概念。下次当你为那些不分享你的背景知识的人分解这些概念时，使用 Linky 和 ​​Hazel 怎么样？事实上，您甚至可以借用 Cartografos Group 新书的页面：Admiral Bash’s Island Adventure with Phippy and Friends。 

您是毕业项目的维护者吗？想捐赠角色吗？ 在此处了解更多信息并打造 Phippy！

跳过行话

云原生计算是一个令人难以置信的领域，但以非技术人员能够掌握的方式进行解释通常很棘手。即使是经验丰富的工程师有时也会发现这具有挑战性。 CNCF 的存在不仅是为了推进云原生生态系统，也是为了帮助来自不同背景的人们拥抱技术，对它提供的可能性感到兴奋，并最终加入#TeamCloudNative。 

我们知道科技正在变得越来越多样化——这太棒了——但我们希望继续鼓励各行各业的更多人考虑云原生职业。毕竟，有大量证据表明开源的多样性、公平性和包容性 (DEI) 如何对整个社会产生积极影响。 BCG 对全球 1,700 多家公司进行的一项研究表明，多元化可以扩大公司创意和选择的范围，从而促进创新，从而带来更好的财务业绩。我们一起变得更强、更快、更好、更具创新性。

我们继续推动 DEI 的一种方法是改善我们的技术沟通。当我们以一种易于理解、直接的方式谈论技术时，我们为比以往任何时候都更多的人敞开了大门——那些可能认为技术太复杂的人。

Phippy 和朋友们来了

2016 年，Deis（现已成为 Microsoft Azure 的一部分）平台架构师Matt Butcher 正在寻找一种向技术人员和非技术人员解释 Kubernetes 的方法。受到女儿丰富的毛绒动物收藏的启发，他提出了“儿童图解 Kubernetes 指南”的想法。与 Karen Chu 和 Bailey Beougher 一起，他创造了 Phippy，一只黄色的 PHP 应用程序长颈鹿，并让她开始了她的第一次冒险。

2018 年，Phippy 被捐赠给 Linux 基金会，如今，Phippy 角色可以免费用于向其他人传授云原生计算知识。 

Phippy 的使命

从一个不起眼的 PHP 应用程序开始，Phippy 已经帮助成千上万的人迈出了理解云原生计算的第一步——从容器化到自动化。如今，Phippy and Friends 的使命是揭开云原生计算的神秘面纱，并通过书籍、演示文稿甚至 Twitter 帖子，以引人入胜、引人入胜且易于理解的方式解释复杂的概念。

这不仅仅是睡前故事，您还可以在日常工作中自由使用 Phippy 和 Friends 角色，尤其是当您的任务是向不具有相同技术背景的人解释团队的项目和产品时。 

Phippy 抵押品

Phippy 和朋友们的角色（Phippy、Goldie、Captain Kube、Zee、Hazel 和 Linky）已获得知识共享署名许可 (CC-BY) 的许可，这意味着您可以出于任何目的（甚至是商业目的）对材料进行重新混合、转换和构建。如果您使用这些字符，请包含文本“phippy.io”以提供归属信息（在网上，请包含指向 https://phippy.io 的链接）。

您可以下载 CNCF 图稿 repo 中的 Phippy 角色图像，格式为 svg、png 和 ai，彩色、黑色和白色。

别忘了，CNCF 商店还提供 Phippy 和朋友们的周边商品，包括 Phippy 书籍的硬拷贝，其中包括全新书籍 Admiral Bash's Island Adventure 和毛绒玩具。

item.title: 【Flux trusted by Amazon, D2iQ, Microsoft, Red Hat, VMware, and Weaveworks】Flux 受到 Amazon、D2iQ、Microsoft、Red Hat、VMware 和 Weaveworks 的信任
item.description:
【

Project post by Tamao Nakahara, Head of Developer Experience, Weaveworks and Flux community manager

On October 20, 6 major vendors are joining to demonstrate their backing of a major CNCF project – Flux.

Top Cloud and GitOps vendors – Microsoft, Amazon, VMware, D2iQ, Weaveworks, and Red Hat – all trust CNCF Flux to deliver GitOps to their enterprise customers.

We’ve heard how major companies such as Alibaba, State Farm, and Deutsche Telekom have shared their success using Flux. There are new adopters everyday who add themselves to the list of happy Flux users and share their love for Flux, the most recent being the U.S. Department of Defense.

Flux’s success is at another level now as the CTOs, GMs, and others will attest on October 20 of how they trust Flux to deliver GitOps to their end customers. Many of the customers of these vendors simply want GitOps to work and they may not be opinionated about what is under the hood. So it’s important for companies such as Amazon, D2iQ, Microsoft, Red Hat, VMware, and Weaveworks to use a trusted CNCF technology that will help them deliver enterprise-level quality, security, and reliability.

For practitioners and customers looking to move forward with GitOps, the event will be a showcase of enterprise offerings to make the next step easier:

  • Amazon EKS Anywhere
  • D2iQ DKP Kommander
  • Microsoft Azure Arc Kubernetes
  • VMware Tanzu Application Platform
  • Weaveworks Weave GitOps
  • Red Hat OpenShift

Brendan Burns (Kubernetes co-creator) and Ihor Dvoretskyi(CNCF Developer Advocate) will be on stage as well to celebrate this momentous event of a CNCF project that is also swiftly completing the steps toward graduation.

For long-time Flux fans, the latest technological updates are part of why they have trusted Flux for its security and reliability, and how the project helps with velocity, time and money savings, and more. Here are some recent highlights from the Flux project:  

  • API Stability: The API has been stable since June 2021 and continues to improve. Contractors at the U.S. Department of Defense recently shared how it was one of many reasons that Flux was a clear choice for them.
  • Cloud Support: The team added support for various cloud offerings, so your general tooling should just fall into place (ssh/gpg key formats, git submodules, extended SOPS support) and full support for mixed-arch clusters. Our support for vendors has included adding Flux to OpenShift OperatorHub so that users can now get GitOps with OpenShift via Flux. 
  • Improved performance: By using Server-Side Apply (available since Kubernetes 1.18) for reconciliation, we were able to improve performance (CPU, memory, network, FD usage) and reduce the number of calls to the Kubernetes API. 
  • Using CRDs and CRs together: Because of this work using Server-Side Apply, Flux can now validate and reconcile sources that contain both CRDs and CRs together. 
  • Improved reliability, drift detection, and observability: Flux can now detect and report drift more reliably between the desired state (git, s3, etc) and cluster state by leveraging Server-Side Apply. Users can now wait for all applied resources to become ready without the need to fill in the health checks list. This improves the overall observability of the reconciliation process by reporting in real-time the garbage collection and health assessment actions.
  • Working integrations: Integrations are critical for users, so fluxcd/pkg now encapsulates best practices for GitOps controllers. 
  • Compatibility: It has been our priority for Flux to work well with all the tools that you use regularly. Now our notification-controller (a component of the microservices-designed Flux project) can send alerts and notifications on almost all major protocols. 
  • Flagger: The list of new features has grown and it’s in the process of being rebased on top of Flux controllers.

Because of this, the Flux community continues to grow in terms of users, contributors, and maintainers. Hear from some of its greatest supporters on October 20 at www.gitopsdays.com. 

】

Weaveworks 和 Flux 社区经理、开发者体验主管 Tamao Nakahara 发表的项目帖子

10 月 20 日，6 家主要供应商齐聚一堂，展示他们对 CNCF 重大项目 - Flux 的支持。

顶级云和 GitOps 供应商 - Microsoft、Amazon、VMware、D2iQ、Weaveworks 和 Red Hat - 都信任 CNCF Flux 向其企业客户提供 GitOps。

我们听说阿里巴巴、State Farm 和德国电信等大公司如何分享他们使用 Flux 取得的成功。每天都有新的采用者将自己添加到 Flux 快乐用户列表并分享他们对 Flux 的热爱，最近的一个是 美国国防部。

Flux 的成功现在达到了另一个水平，因为首席技术官、总经理和其他人将在 10 月 20 日证明他们如何信任 Flux 向最终客户交付 GitOps。这些供应商的许多客户只是希望 GitOps 能够工作，他们可能对幕后的内容没有意见。因此，对于 Amazon、D2iQ、Microsoft、Red Hat、VMware 和 Weaveworks 等公司来说，使用值得信赖的 CNCF 技术非常重要，这将帮助他们提供企业级的质量、安全性和可靠性。

对于希望推进 GitOps 的从业者和客户来说，该活动将展示企业产品，让下一步变得更容易：

  • Amazon EKS Anywhere
  • D2iQ DKP Kommander
  • Microsoft Azure Arc Kubernetes
  • VMware Tanzu 应用平台
  • Weaveworks Weave GitOps
  • Red Hat OpenShift

Brendan Burns（Kubernetes 联合创始人）和 Ihor Dvoretskyi（CNCF 开发者倡导者）也将登台庆祝 CNCF 项目的这一重大事件，该项目也正在迅速完成毕业步骤。

对于 Flux 的长期粉丝来说，最新的技术更新是他们信任 Flux 安全性和可靠性的部分原因，以及该项目如何帮助提高速度、节省时间和金钱等。以下是 Flux 项目的一些最新亮点：  

  • API 稳定性：API 自 2021 年 6 月以来一直保持稳定，并持续改进。 美国国防部最近分享了 Flux 成为他们明确选择的众多原因之一。
  • 云支持：团队添加了对各种云产品的支持，因此您的通用工具应该就位（ssh/gpg 密钥格式、git 子模块、扩展 SOPS 支持）并完全支持混合架构集群。我们对供应商的支持包括将 Flux 添加到 OpenShift OperatorHub，以便用户现在可以通过 Flux 使用 OpenShift 获取 GitOps。 
  • 提高性能：通过使用服务器端应用（自 Kubernetes 1.18 起可用）进行协调，我们能够提高性能（CPU、内存、网络、FD 使用）并减少对 Kubernetes API 的调用次数。 
  • 同时使用 CRD 和 CR：由于使用了服务器端应用，Flux 现在可以验证并协调同时包含 CRD 和 CR 的源。 
  • 改进的可靠性、偏差检测和可观察性： Flux 现在可以利用服务器端应用，更可靠地检测和报告所需状态（git、s3 等）和集群状态之间的偏差。用户现在可以等待所有应用的资源准备就绪，而无需填写运行状况检查列表。通过实时报告垃圾收集和运行状况评估操作，这提高了协调过程的整体可观察性。
  • 工作集成：集成对于用户至关重要，因此 Fluxcd/pkg 现在封装了 GitOps 控制器的最佳实践。 
  • 兼容性：Flux 的首要任务是与您经常使用的所有工具良好配合。现在，我们的通知控制器（微服务设计的 Flux 项目的一个组件）可以在几乎所有主要协议上发送警报和通知。 
  • Flagger：新功能列表已经增加，并且正在基于 Flux 控制器进行重新构建。

因此，Flux 社区的用户、贡献者和维护者不断增长。 10 月 20 日，请访问 www.gitopsdays.com，聆听一些最伟大支持者的意见。

item.title: 【MITRE ATT&CK Matrix for Kubernetes】Kubernetes 的 MITRE ATT&CK 矩阵
item.description:
【

Guest post originally published on the Magalix blog by Rahul Sharma

Overview

Last year, CNCF reported an extraordinary 300% increase in Kubernetes adoption from 23% in March 2016 to 92% in July 2020. The data clearly outlines Kubernetes as the industry standard for container orchestration. But the study also reports Kubernetes security remains as one of the top 3 challenges. As organizations accelerate their adoption of containers, they must invest in safeguarding this critical infrastructure.

A typical large Kubernetes cluster is a highly distributed environment with many deployed applications. There can be misconfigurations and vulnerabilities that expose various services to unintended users. An adversary can exploit these gaps to gain access and perform malicious activities. Malware and Spyware programs can be executed using containers or sidecars etc. The ephemeral nature of containers makes it hard to monitor and trace these exploits. Securing Kubernetes and cloud-native architecture using best practices is a good starting place, but it does not provide guidance to identify and mitigate attacks.

What is MITRE ATT&CK Matrix?

MITRE ATT&CK framework is a knowledge base of techniques and tactics attackers use to infiltrate and attack Kubernetes clusters. An attacker usually strategizes how to infiltrate a cluster and perform damage by following the stages that entail an attack lifecycle. The attacker must progress through each of these stages for the attack to be successful. The threat matrix identifies the tactics used at various stages of a cyberattack. 

MITRE ATT&CK framework tactics include the following: 

  1. Initial Access.
  2. Execution.
  3. Persistence.
  4. Privilege escalation.
  5. Defense evasion.
  6. Credential access.
  7. Discovery.
  8. Lateral movement.
  9. Collection.
  10. Impact.

In the above matrix, each column represents a different attack stage, and each item in each column represents a tactic employed by attackers during that stage. The categorization is intended to help organizations determine their attack surface and adopt suitable detection and mitigation strategies.

Understanding the ATT&CK matrix also helps the security teams implement strategies and technologies to contain the attack. Blocking adversaries at any stage breaks the attack and mitigates the incurred damage. Thus prevention must occur at each stage to block the attackers’ ability to access and move laterally within the cluster or steal sensitive data. Thus the matrix also represents a layered model for security enforcement. Layering your security in this way prevents one weak point from jeopardizing the cluster.

1- Initial Access

Initial access is the first stage of the attack. This tactic references the attacker’s objective of gaining access to Kubernetes clusters by either compromising a component within the Kubernetes control plane that handles cluster management or various resources within the cluster – for example, a running application component on a worker node, pushing malware-infected images, etc. 

Techniques in this early stage are focused on unpatched vulnerabilities or credentials leaks. Organizations must perform periodic Kubernetes upgrades, implement vulnerability scanning and limit access by leveraging Kubernetes RBAC. Implementing these measures dramatically reduces an organization’s attack surface.

2- Execution

During execution, the attacker runs code inside of a Kubernetes cluster to accomplish their goals. They can exploit an application vulnerability, access a pod, deploy a sidecar or use other means to execute the malicious code.

This tactic involves executing workloads within a cluster. Isolating workloads by creating separate namespaces is an essential step for mitigation. Furthermore, organizations can implement Pod Security Policies to govern the execution of workloads.

3- Persistence

Maintaining access to a compromised target is a key tactic used by attackers. This is most commonly done by exploiting a backdoor. In the world of Kubernetes, there are many access points to allow access to its resources. 

Attackers often execute an innocuous HTTP request to one of their machines and then upgrade this connection to an interactive remote shell. These commands are persisted as a script on the Kubernetes Node using hostPath volume mount. The script is then executed by containers scheduled using Kubernetes Cron, thus providing a persistent connection to the attacker. A Kubernetes cluster can be protected by implementing Pod Security Policy, to deny host mount and  Network Policies to control how traffic is routed in and out of the cluster.

4- Privilege Escalation

Attackers use the privilege escalation tactic to obtain higher privileges in the environment than they currently have. This can include accessing a node through a container, gaining privileges within a cluster, or even using cloud resources.

Organizations must apply the fundamental principle of least privilege that grants only required privileges and no more. To prevent security issues, it is recommended that you do not run privileged containers in your environment. Instead, provide granular permissions and capabilities to the required containers.

5- Defense Evasion

Defense Evasion techniques are focused on concealing adversary actions intended to avoid detection. This includes tactics such as deleting evidence of an attacker’s presence or obfuscating how access to a resource was gained. 

Audit logs allow administrators to view all security events in the Kubernetes cluster. Make sure to enable and monitor audit logs for anomalous or unwanted API calls, especially any authorization failures. Additionally, administrators should minimize container access to underlying nodes, especially in the Kubernetes control plane, by restricting host mounts. 

6- Credential Access

Having established their presence within the cluster, elevated permissions, planted backdoors, and evaded defenses, the attacker is now ready to look after data and credentials. This may include secrets that are part of a cluster, database, application credentials part of a program, or even cloud credentials for managed clusters.

It is often not recommended to mount sensitive information on all Nodes. Alternatively, teams must use Node pools effectively to schedule sensitive and secret information for only the required workloads.

7- Discovery

An adversary uses this type of attack to gather information about the cluster components and their internal networking. The information allows them to exploit vulnerabilities in the deployed services to move further to applications and databases.  

The tactic can be mitigated by limiting access to sensitive assets using the least privileged credentials, Pod security policies, and network policies.

8- Lateral Movement

With malware installed and traces removed, attackers now can access and control all nodes in the cluster. The purpose of these techniques is to allow an adversary to access and control remote systems on a network and possibly include the execution of tools on the remote system. 

All Pods in a cluster can communicate with each other unless restricted by NetworkPolicy. Attackers can use the container service account credentials available on the Pod, to connect to the Kubernetes API server to determine cluster workloads. Then they can connect to the other containers deployed in the cluster. Organizations must enable RBAC to limit cluster access and enforce Network Policies to control how traffic is routed.

9- Collection

The tactic consists of techniques adversaries use for gathering information, besides the compromised cluster. In Kubernetes, images are downloaded from a private registry like ECR, Quay, etc. If access credentials of the registry are compromised, they can access the registry to manipulate all container images. Organizations can mitigate the attack by having read-only policies for the registry credentials used in Kubernetes.

10- Impact

Disruption or destruction of resources within the target environment is the ultimate goal of an attacker. Data destruction, resource hijacking, and denial of service are some of these techniques.

An attacker can scale down deployments, remove statefulSets, delete volumes, terminate running pods, drain nodes, etc, impacting your business workflows. They can also trigger a denial of service attack by shutting down Kubernetes components like control Plane. Alternatively, Kubernetes resources can be used for crypto mining by adding new containers for this activity.

Conclusion

Disrupting cyber attacks involves several stages of the attack lifecycle. If they cannot successfully exploit vulnerabilities, they cannot penetrate and will not obtain remote control over the cluster. The MITRE ATT&CK framework provides the necessary knowledge on how such attacks happen in the real world. The framework helps to hardener your cluster security with a layered defense approach.  

Magalix Policy Enforcement Platform has 100s out-of-the-box policies and templates – PCI DSS, Application-Based Policies, and MITRE ATT&CK – enabling companies to hit the ground running. 

】

客座帖子最初由 Rahul Sharma 在Magalix 博客上发布

概述

去年，CNCF 报告，Kubernetes 的采用率显着增加了 300%，从 2016 年 3 月的 23% 增加到 2020 年 7 月的 92%。这些数据清楚地表明 Kubernetes 是容器编排的行业标准。但该研究还报告称，Kubernetes 安全性仍然是三大挑战之一。随着组织加速采用容器，他们必须投资保护这一关键基础设施。

典型的大型 Kubernetes 集群是一个高度分布式的环境，部署了许多应用程序。可能存在错误配置和漏洞，从而向非预期用户公开各种服务。对手可以利用这些漏洞来获取访问权限并执行恶意活动。恶意软件和间谍软件程序可以使用容器或 sidecar 等来执行。容器的短暂性使得监视和跟踪这些漏洞变得困难。 使用最佳实践保护 Kubernetes 和云原生架构是一个很好的起点，但它不提供识别和缓解攻击的指导。

什么是 MITRE ATT&CK 矩阵？

MITRE ATT&CK框架是攻击者用于渗透和攻击Kubernetes集群的技术和策略的知识库。攻击者通常通过遵循攻击生命周期的各个阶段来制定如何渗透集群并造成损害的策略。攻击者必须完成每个阶段才能使攻击成功。威胁矩阵确定了网络攻击各个阶段使用的策略。 

MITRE ATT&CK 框架策略包括以下内容：

  1. 初始访问。
  2. 执行。
  3. 持久性。
  4. 权限升级。
  5. 防御规避。
  6. 凭据访问。
  7. 发现。
  8. 横向运动。
  9. 收藏。
  10. 影响。

在上面的矩阵中，每一列代表不同的攻击阶段，每一列中的每一项代表攻击者在该阶段所采用的策略。分类旨在帮助组织确定其攻击面并采取适当的检测和缓解策略。

了解 ATT&CK 矩阵还有助于安全团队实施策略和技术来遏制攻击。在任何阶段阻止对手都会破坏攻击并减轻所造成的损害。因此，必须在每个阶段进行预防，以阻止攻击者访问和在集群内横向移动或窃取敏感数据的能力。因此，该矩阵还代表了安全实施的分层模型。以这种方式分层安全可以防止一个弱点危及集群。

1- 初始访问

初始访问是攻击的第一阶段。这种策略涉及攻击者的目标，即通过破坏 Kubernetes 控制平面中处理集群管理的组件或集群内的各种资源来获取对 Kubernetes 集群的访问权限，例如工作节点上正在运行的应用程序组件、推送受恶意软件感染的图像等。

早期阶段的技术主要集中于未修补的漏洞或凭据泄露。组织必须定期执行 Kubernetes 升级、实施漏洞扫描并利用 Kubernetes RBAC 限制访问。实施这些措施可显着减少组织的攻击面。

2-执行

在执行过程中，攻击者在 Kubernetes 集群内运行代码以实现其目标。他们可以利用应用程序漏洞、访问 Pod、部署 Sidecar 或使用其他方式执行恶意代码。

此策略涉及在集群内执行工作负载。通过创建单独的命名空间来隔离工作负载是缓解问题的重要步骤。此外，组织可以实施Pod 安全策略来管理工作负载的执行。

3-持久化

保持对受感染目标的访问是攻击者使用的关键策略。这通常是通过利用后门来完成的。在 Kubernetes 的世界中，有许多访问点可以访问其资源。 

攻击者经常向他们的一台机器执行无害的 HTTP 请求，然后升级此 conn交互式远程 shell 的部分。这些命令使用 hostPath 卷挂载作为脚本保留在 Kubernetes 节点上。然后，该脚本由使用 Kubernetes Cron 调度的容器执行，从而为攻击者提供持久连接。可以通过实施Pod 安全策略来保护 Kubernetes 集群，以拒绝主机挂载，并通过网络策略来控制流量的路由方式并脱离集群。

4- 权限升级

攻击者使用权限提升策略在环境中获取比当前更高的权限。这可以包括通过容器访问节点、获得集群内的权限，甚至使用云资源。

组织必须应用最小权限的基本原则，即仅授予所需的权限，而不再授予更多权限。为了防止安全问题，建议您不要在您的环境中运行特权容器。相反，为所需的容器提供精细的权限和功能。

5-防御规避

防御规避技术的重点是隐藏对手以避免被发现的行为。这包括删除攻击者存在的证据或混淆如何获取资源访问权限等策略。 

审核日志允许管理员查看 Kubernetes 集群中的所有安全事件。确保启用并监控审核日志以发现异常或不需要的 API 调用，尤其是任何授权失败。此外，管理员应通过限制主机挂载来最大程度地减少容器对底层节点的访问，尤其是在 Kubernetes 控制平面中。 

6-凭据访问

在集群中建立存在、提升权限、植入后门并规避防御后，攻击者现在已准备好监视数据和凭据。这可能包括属于集群、数据库、程序的应用程序凭据的一部分的秘密，甚至是托管集群的云凭据。

通常不建议在所有节点上安装敏感信息。或者，团队必须有效地使用节点池来仅为所需的工作负载安排敏感和秘密信息。

7-发现

攻击者使用此类攻击来收集有关集群组件及其内部网络的信息。这些信息使他们能够利用已部署服务中的漏洞进一步转向应用程序和数据库。 ＆nbsp;

可以通过使用最低特权凭证、Pod 安全策略和网络策略来限制对敏感资产的访问来缓解这种策略。

8-横向运动

安装恶意软件并删除痕迹后，攻击者现在可以访问和控制集群中的所有节点。这些技术的目的是允许攻击者访问和控制网络上的远程系统，并可能包括在远程系统上执行工具。 

集群中的所有 Pod 都可以相互通信，除非受到网络策略的限制。攻击者可以使用 Pod 上可用的容器服务帐户凭据连接到 Kubernetes API 服务器来确定集群工作负载。然后它们可以连接到集群中部署的其他容器。组织必须启用 RBAC 来限制集群访问并强制执行网络策略来控制流量的路由方式。

9-集合

除了受损的集群之外，该策略还包括对手用于收集信息的技术。在 Kubernetes 中，镜像是从 ECR、Quay 等私有注册中心下载的。如果注册中心的访问凭证被泄露，他们可以访问注册中心来操纵所有容器镜像。组织可以通过对 Kubernetes 中使用的注册表凭据制定只读策略来缓解攻击。

10-影响

破坏或破坏目标环境中的资源是攻击者的最终目标。数据破坏、资源劫持和拒绝服务都是其中的一些技术。

攻击者可以缩减部署、删除 statefulSet、删除卷、终止运行的 Pod、耗尽节点等，从而影响您的业务工作流程。他们还可以通过关闭控制平面等 Kubernetes 组件来触发拒绝服务攻击。或者，通过为此活动添加新容器，可以将 Kubernetes 资源用于加密货币挖掘。

结论

破坏性网络攻击涉及攻击生命周期的多个阶段。如果他们不能成功利用漏洞，就无法渗透，也无法获得对集群的远程控制。 MITRE ATT&CK 框架提供了有关此类攻击如何在现实世界中发生的必要知识。该框架通过分层防御方法帮助强化集群安全性。  

Magalix 政策执行平台 拥有 100 多个开箱即用的政策和模板 – ；PCI DSS、基于应用程序的策略和MITRE ATT&CK – 帮助公司迅速开展业务。

item.title: 【Best practices for edge security】边缘安全的最佳实践
item.description:
【

Guest post originally published on Snapt’s blog by Craig Risi

In the corporate tech fight against security threats, there is perhaps no struggle more difficult than dealing with Edge Security. While companies can put in extensive security protocols and systems to protect much of their data, applications, and core data centers or cloud servers, monitoring the security of an entire organization and the people working for it is incredibly difficult.  

What is Edge Security?

Edge Security refers to the security of all those corporate resources that exist outside of their protected data centers. This includes all users, devices, and even external applications that may still have access to the corporate networks in some way and therefore are still liable to risk should they ever become compromised.   

Why is Edge Security difficult?

What makes Edge Security so difficult is that the number of variables to contend with is significantly higher. Whereas companies can control to a large degree the security around their core systems with strict firewalls and access control protocols, it’s difficult to police every little person and resource that has access to your corporate networks. However, that doesn’t mean that it can’t be done, which is why companies need to adopt the following best practices to reduce the risk to security at the edge. 

1. Prioritize the 5 Ps

To start with, it’s worth identifying the 5 P’s of Edge Security: People, Policies, Process, Products, and Proof. You must fully understand all the critical aspects that form your organization’s “edge” and ensure they are prioritized correctly in addressing the security gaps that could occur at each of these levels. 

First off, and always critical, is people. There’s a need for individual training and reinforcement of training, as well as a cultural mindset. There is no point in trying to put in place extensive technical measures if you can’t change the way people think about security and the seriousness with which teams take edge security. 

Policies and procedures are the governance that enables and reminds people to maintain vigilance. This is about creating clear ownership and accountability for different aspects of Edge Security and making sure that the right measures are implemented with specific people taking responsibility for the implementation and enforcement of them.

The process includes the things people must do to fully mitigate risks. Companies mustn’t wait for incidents to occur before figuring out how to deal with them. Rather these processes should be clearly documented with plans for different incident levels so that the instructions are clear on how certain personnel, systems, and the business as a whole need to respond. 

Products might be the most challenging of the five Ps. It’s hard for IT organizations to make sense of what an end-to-end cybersecurity solution looks like. From hardware to software, from device to server, from network access to infrastructure protection, and from OT to IT, security needs to be considered across the board with synchronization between all the components. This requires an understanding of the communication needs between different systems and what level of security and access control is required across them. 

The proof involves the regular testing of products, processes, policies and procedures, and people to ensure cyber risk is truly mitigated, or to find vulnerabilities and shore up those weaknesses. The trick here though is not just having once-off events to identify these areas, but to put tools and teams in place to do this regularly. Without this regular cadence of testing and remediation, cybersecurity strategies can and will quickly become outdated and ineffective.

2. Separate Networks

Perhaps most critically is for companies to build separate networks into the organization that all serve a different purpose and require rigorous VPNs should they need to communicate with each other. Many companies may want to have one big network where all systems are connected to create efficiency in operation, but the reality is that most people won’t need access to certain systems and many systems can often operate independently of others. 

Rather, companies should look to keep certain critical systems completely separate from the rest of the organization. The majority of the business operations should work on a network that doesn’t connect to the critical systems in any way. Additionally, they may configure a guest network for personal devices, but again it needs to be separate and disconnected from the other networks.

Having separate networks might prove inconvenient for people or systems that may need to operate on both networks, because this will require users to navigate VPNs with strict rules that allow only specific addresses. However, by completely isolating your critical operations from your Edge you greatly reduce the risk of any compromises in your edge network from affecting critical systems and this makes those painful processes worth it.  

3. Zero Trust

This might sound difficult for many companies wanting to build trust into their business, but when it comes to edge security it’s best to consider a Zero Trust policy for all users and devices on a network. 

This can prove especially frustrating to employees who would like to make use of their USB ports to transfer personal data or connect to devices on outside networks. But the truth is that these can open up a company to too many risks and even well-intentioned employees can make mistakes. 

Companies should rather ensure that device and network permissions are as restrictive as possible to keep the risk of a security breach as low as possible. Should people need to breach those measures for whatever reason, then they should use a device on a separate network for that purpose, or consult with a security expert to determine the risk. 

Of course the same applies to training, and even though most staff are sick and tired of the same regular security training we are constantly fed, companies can’t assume people know what to do and so need to continue enforcing this training on people for the foreseeable future.

4. Contingency and Redundancy

A lot of risk at the edge can be avoided if companies built in the right contingency and redundancy measures to allow systems to operate safely when others are compromised. It may be expensive to have systems that exist on more than one network, but it allows for companies to easily isolate compromised networks and ensure that the company remains operational with the risk of being compromised significantly reduced. 

The reason many companies don’t do this is due to the sheer cost of this process. It is all too easy for some companies to dismiss this cost as a wasted expense because it means keeping more networks and systems operational than is strictly required, especially when talking about systems operating on the edge that doesn’t appear critical. However, in the event of a serious security failure, these companies will be glad to have a redundant secure network to enable them to remain operational.

5. Proactive Monitoring

It’s not just a matter of having rules and gateways in place to protect your company’s Edge security. Companies must maintain visibility of what is going on across the organization. 

Monitoring can be a sensitive topic, especially when it comes to monitoring individual machines on the network. The point though is not to check up on what people are doing, but for security teams to know when an unusual or suspicious event happens so that they can contain the situation before serious consequences occur. 

Outside of company machines, it’s also important to have monitoring across all network access points, firewalls, and corporate servers to check the traffic going across the corporate networks, and to send additional notifications to security teams when abnormalities occur.

6. Patching

We’ve spoken about patching often and for good reason. Most patches contain important security updates, and companies delaying these patches for too long simply open themselves up to the vulnerable for longer. 

Patching can feel inconvenient due to the high number and frequency in which they are required, but they are a key component to keeping organizations secure and should never be compromised. Companies should build in the appropriate times and processes to allow all devices and applications to be updated, especially at an edge level, and put measures in place to exclude devices and applications without the latest updates from accessing specific networks. 

It’s perhaps worth mentioning that as inconvenient as patching can be, the more regular you do it, the smaller the updates are and therefore the less risky each patch becomes. Teams that allow long intervals between patching might inadvertently increase the risk of making the collective updates bigger than intended, which increases the amount of testing required and the number of things that could go wrong. 

How can ADCs enhance your corporate Edge Security?

Protecting the edge is often more complex than that of core systems given the increased number of variables involved. This is why it is so important to make use of ADCs and WAFs to manage this security. Application Delivery Controllers (ADCs) with Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) can operate on every critical access point or server on a network and can scan all the relevant traffic, block unwanted communication proactively and adjust the flow of traffic to other resources to protect against security breaches. 

ADCs are a critical part of any company’s edge computing security because their ability to react to security concerns proactively greatly reduces the risk of breaches and cuts out potential vulnerabilities and attacks before they become a problem. ADCs can do all this far quicker than any well-skilled team will be able to. 

Ensuring that ADCs are set up at all vital points on your corporate network is a necessity for any company wanting to take its endpoint security seriously.

Conclusion

It’s clear that vital systems and all confidential data need to be well protected, but that is not the end of the risks, as every component and system of a corporate network needs to be protected as well. 

Much like every other aspect of security, companies cannot afford to compromise on their edge security and should build a strict set of measures and implement the right systems to mitigate the risks. There is no one quick solution to the problem though, and it’s important that companies take a multi-pronged approach to ensure they keep every aspect of their edge secure.

】

客座帖子最初由 Craig Risi 在 Snapt 博客上发布

在企业技术对抗安全威胁的斗争中，也许没有比处理边缘安全更困难的斗争了。虽然公司可以采用广泛的安全协议和系统来保护其大部分数据、应用程序以及核心数据中心或云服务器，但监控整个组织及其工作人员的安全性却非常困难。  

什么是边缘安全？

边缘安全是指存在于受保护数据中心之外的所有企业资源的安全。这包括所有用户、设备，甚至可能仍然可以以某种方式访问​​公司网络的外部应用程序，因此如果受到威胁，仍然可能面临风险。   

为什么边缘安全很困难？

边缘安全之所以如此困难，是因为需要应对的变量数量明显增多。虽然公司可以通过严格的防火墙和访问控制协议在很大程度上控制其核心系统的安全性，但很难监管有权访问公司网络的每个小人和资源。然而，这并不意味着它不能做到，这就是为什么公司需要采用以下最佳实践来降低边缘安全风险。 

1.优先考虑 5 个 P

首先，有必要确定边缘安全的 5P：人员、策略、流程、产品和证明。您必须充分了解构成组织“优势”的所有关键方面，并确保在解决每个级别可能出现的安全漏洞时正确确定它们的优先级。 

首先且始终至关重要的是人。需要个人培训和强化培训，以及文化心态。如果无法改变人们对安全的看法以及团队对待边缘安全的重视程度，那么尝试采取广泛的技术措施就没有意义。 

政策和程序是使人们能够保持警惕并提醒人们保持警惕的治理。这是关于为边缘安全的不同方面建立明确的所有权和问责制，并确保实施正确的措施，并由特定人员负责实施和执行。

该流程包括人们为充分降低风险必须采取的措施。公司不能等到事件发生才弄清楚如何处理来对付他们。相反，这些流程应该清楚地记录不同事件级别的计划，以便清楚地说明某些人员、系统和整个业务需要如何响应。 

产品可能是 5 个 P 中最具挑战性的。 IT 组织很难理解端到端网络安全解决方案是什么样的。从硬件到软件，从设备到服务器，从网络访问到基础设施保护，从OT到IT，需要全面考虑安全性并在所有组件之间进行同步。这需要了解不同系统之间的通信需求以及它们之间需要什么级别的安全和访问控制。 

证明涉及对产品、流程、政策和程序以及人员进行定期测试，以确保网络风险真正得到缓解，或发现漏洞并弥补这些弱点。不过，这里的技巧不仅仅是举办一次性活动来识别这些领域，而是部署工具和团队来定期执行此操作。如果没有这种定期的测试和修复节奏，网络安全策略可能很快就会过时且无效。

2.独立的网络

对于公司来说，最关键的可能是在组织中构建独立的网络，这些网络都有不同的目的，并且如果需要相互通信，则需要严格的 VPN。许多公司可能希望拥有一个所有系统都连接起来的大型网络，以提高运营效率，但现实是大多数人不需要访问某些系统，而且许多系统通常可以独立于其他系统运行。 

相反，公司应该设法将某些关键系统与组织的其他部分完全分开。大多数业务运营应该在不以任何方式连接到关键系统的网络上进行。此外，他们可以为个人设备配置访客网络，但同样需要将其与其他网络分开并断开连接。

拥有单独的网络可能会给需要在两个网络上运行的人员或系统带来不便，因为这将要求用户使用仅允许特定地址的严格规则来导航 VPN。然而，通过将关键操作与边缘完全隔离，可以大大降低边缘网络中任何妥协影响关键系统的风险，这使得这些痛苦的过程变得值得。  

3.零信任

对于许多希望在业务中建立信任的公司来说，这听起来可能很困难，但在边缘安全方面，最好考虑对网络上的所有用户和设备实施零信任策略。 

这可以证明特殊性对于想要利用 USB 端口传输个人数据或连接到外部网络设备的员工来说，这无疑是令人沮丧的。但事实是，这些可能会让公司面临太多风险，即使是善意的员工也可能会犯错误。 

公司应该确保设备和网络权限尽可能受到限制，以尽可能降低安全漏洞的风险。如果人们出于某种原因需要违反这些措施，那么他们应该为此目的使用单独网络上的设备，或者咨询安全专家以确定风险。 

当然，这同样适用于培训，尽管大多数员工已经厌倦了我们不断接受的定期安全培训，但公司不能假设人们知道该做什么，因此需要在可预见的未来继续对人们实施这种培训。

4.应急和冗余

如果公司建立正确的应急措施和冗余措施，让系统在其他系统受到威胁时安全运行，则可以避免很多边缘风险。拥有多个网络上存在的系统可能会很昂贵，但它允许公司轻松隔离受感染的网络，并确保公司保持运营，同时显着降低受感染的风险。 

许多公司不这样做的原因是这个过程的成本高昂。对于一些公司来说，很容易将这笔成本视为浪费费用，因为这意味着保持比严格要求更多的网络和系统运行，特别是在谈论在边缘运行的系统时，这些系统似乎并不重要。然而，如果发生严重的安全故障，这些公司将很高兴拥有一个冗余的安全网络来使他们能够保持运营。

5.主动监控

这不仅仅是制定规则和网关来保护公司边缘安全的问题。公司必须保持整个组织内正在发生的事情的可见性。 

监控可能是一个敏感话题，尤其是在监控网络上的单个计算机时。但重点不是检查人们在做什么，而是让安全团队知道何时发生异常或可疑事件，以便他们能够在严重后果发生之前控制局势。 

在公司机器之外，对所有网络接入点、防火墙和公司服务器进行监控也很重要，以检查通过公司网络的流量，并发送额外的通知来发现问题。当异常发生时，安全团队。

6.修补

我们经常谈论修补，这是有充分理由的。大多数补丁都包含重要的安全更新，而公司推迟这些补丁太久只会更长时间地向漏洞开放。 

由于需要修补的数量和频率较高，修补可能会感觉不方便，但它们是确保组织安全的关键组成部分，绝不应受到损害。公司应建立适当的时间和流程，以允许更新所有设备和应用程序，尤其是在边缘级别，并采取措施排除没有最新更新的设备和应用程序访问特定网络。 

也许值得一提的是，尽管打补丁可能很不方便，但打补丁越频繁，更新就越小，因此每个补丁的风险就越小。允许修补之间较长时间间隔的团队可能会无意中增加集体更新超出预期的风险，从而增加了所需的测试量和可能出错的事情的数量。 

ADC 如何增强您的企业边缘安全？

鉴于涉及的变量数量增加，保护边缘通常比保护核心系统更复杂。这就是利用 ADC 和 WAF 来管理此安全性如此重要的原因。具有 Web 应用程序防火墙 (WAF) 的应用程序交付控制器 (ADC) 可以在网络上的每个关键接入点或服务器上运行，并且可以扫描所有相关流量、主动阻止不需要的通信并调整流向其他资源的流量以防止安全漏洞。 

ADC 是任何公司边缘计算安全的关键部分，因为它们主动响应安全问题的能力大大降低了违规风险，并在潜在漏洞和攻击成为问题之前将其消除。 ADC 可以比任何熟练团队更快地完成这一切。 

对于任何想要认真对待端点安全的公司来说，确保在公司网络的所有重要点都设置 ADC 是必要的。

结论

很明显，重要系统和所有机密数据都需要得到很好的保护，但这并不是风险的终结，因为企业网络的每个组件和系统也需要受到保护。 

就像安全的其他方面一样，公司不能妥协重视其边缘安全，应建立一套严格的措施并实施正确的系统来减轻风险。不过，这个问题没有一个快速的解决方案，重要的是公司必须采取多管齐下的方法来确保其边缘各个方面的安全。

item.title: 【CNCF’s KubeCon + CloudNativeCon diversity scholarship will now be named the Dan Kohn Diversity Scholarship】CNCF 的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 多元化奖学金现将命名为 Dan Kohn 多元化奖学金
item.description:
【

We are honored to announce that Cloud Native Computing Foundation’s KubeCon + CloudNativeCon scholarship program will now officially be named the Dan Kohn Diversity Scholarship. 

Dan was a titan of the open source community with a brilliant mind and gentle soul. He was at the helm of CNCF for over five years and provided the guidance that led the project to become one of the most successful open source foundations of all time. Dan believed wholeheartedly that a diverse community made for a stronger and more vibrant and innovative community, and thanks to his leadership, CNCF has been able to offer over 1,518 scholarships to date. 

These scholarships have helped countless underrepresented individuals become valued members of #teamcloudnative. In fact, Dan was one of the first proponents of these scholarships, so it’s only fitting that we honor his legacy by offering these scholarships in his name.    

Applicants must be from traditionally underrepresented and/or marginalized groups in the technology and/or open source communities who are unable to attend without some financial assistance. Including, but not limited to persons identifying as: LGBTQIA+, women, persons of color, and/or persons with disabilities. 

Dan’s presence is felt everyday across the CNCF and open source communities. We are proud to honor him and his family in this way and our hope is that these scholarship recipients will carry a piece of Dan’s legacy forward with them throughout their careers. 

If you are interested in applying for a scholarship for the upcoming KubeCon + CloudNativeCon + Open Source Summit China 2021 – Virtual, the deadline to apply is November 21, 2021 You can also visit the Dan Kohn memorial site.

】

我们很荣幸地宣布，云原生计算基金会的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 奖学金计划现已正式命名为 Dan Kohn 多元化奖学金。 

Dan 是开源社区的巨人，拥有聪明的头脑和温柔的灵魂。他执掌 CNCF 五年多，并提供指导，使该项目成为有史以来最成功的开源基金会之一。 Dan 全心全意地相信，多元化的社区可以造就一个更强大、更有活力和创新的社区，并且在他的领导下，CNCF 迄今为止已能够提供超过 1,518 项奖学金。 

这些奖学金帮助无数代表性不足的个人成为#teamcloudnative 的重要成员。事实上，丹是这些奖学金的首批支持者之一，因此我们以他的名义提供这些奖学金来纪念他的遗产是再合适不过的了。    

申请人必须来自技术和/或开源社区中传统上代表性不足和/或边缘化的群体，如果没有一些经济援助就无法参加。包括但不限于 LGBTQIA+、女性、有色人种和/或残疾人。 

CNCF 和开源社区每天都能感受到 Dan 的存在。我们很自豪能够以这种方式向他和他的家人致敬，我们希望这些奖学金获得者能够在他们的职业生涯中继承丹的遗产。 

如果您有兴趣为即将举行的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon + 开源峰会中国 2021 – 虚拟申请奖学金，申请截止日期为 2021 年 11 月 21 日。您还可以访问 Dan Kohn 纪念网站。

item.title: 【Flux celebrates major milestone】Flux 庆祝重要里程碑
item.description:
【

At KubeCon + CloudNativeCon NA 2021, the Flux team is celebrating a major milestone!

Top Cloud and GitOps vendors – Microsoft, Amazon, VMware, D2iQ, and Weaveworks – all trust CNCF Flux to deliver GitOps to their enterprise customers!

We’ve heard how major companies such as Alibaba, State Farm, and Deutsche Telekom have shared their success using Flux. There are new adopters everyday who add themselves to the list of happy Flux users and share their love for Flux, the most recent being the U.S. Department of Defense.

Flux’s success is at another level now as the CTOs, GMs, and others will attest on October 20 of how they trust Flux to deliver GitOps. 

Did you know that all of these offerings use Flux for enterprise?

  • Amazon EKS Anywhere
  • D2iQ DKP Kommander
  • Microsoft Azure Arc Kubernetes
  • VMware Tanzu Application Platform
  • Weaveworks Weave GitOps

Brendan Burns (Kubernetes co-creator) and Ihor Dvoretskyi(CNCF Developer Advocate) will be on stage as well to celebrate this momentous event of a CNCF project that is also swiftly completing the steps toward graduation.

What’s new with Flux that continues its success, security, reliability, and trust from these types of companies? 

  • API Stability: The API has been stable since June 2021 and continues to improve. Contractors at the U.S. Department of Defense recently shared how it was one of many reasons that Flux was a clear choice for them.
  • Cloud Support: The team added support for various cloud offerings, so your general tooling should just fall into place (ssh/gpg key formats, git submodules, extended SOPS support) and full support for mixed-arch clusters. Our support for vendors has included adding Flux to OpenShift OperatorHub so that users can now get GitOps with OpenShift via Flux. 
  • Improved performance: By using Server-Side Apply (available since Kubernetes 1.18) for reconciliation, we were able to improve performance (CPU, memory, network, FD usage) and reduce the number of calls to the Kubernetes API. 
  • Using CRDs and CRs together: Because of this work using Server-Side Apply, Flux can now validate and reconcile sources that contain both CRDs and CRs together. 
  • Improved reliability, drift detection, and observability: Flux can now detect and report drift more reliably between the desired state (git, s3, etc) and cluster state by leveraging Server-Side Apply. Users can now wait for all applied resources to become ready without the need to fill in the health checks list. This improves the overall observability of the reconciliation process by reporting in real-time the garbage collection and health assessment actions.
  • Working integrations: Integrations are critical for users, so fluxcd/pkg now encapsulates best practices for GitOps controllers. 
  • Compatibility: It has been our priority for Flux to work well with all the tools that you use regularly. Now our notification-controller (a component of the microservices-designed Flux project) can send alerts and notifications on almost all major protocols. 
  • Flagger: The list of new features has grown and it’s in the process of being rebased on top of Flux controllers.

It is no surprise that, because of this, the Flux community continues to grow in terms of users, contributors, and maintainers. Hear from some of its greatest supporters on October 20 at www.gitopsdays.com.

】

在 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon NA 2021 上，Flux 团队正在庆祝一个重要的里程碑！

顶级云和 GitOps 供应商 - Microsoft、Amazon、VMware、D2iQ 和 Weaveworks - 都信任 CNCF Flux 向其企业客户提供 GitOps！

我们听说阿里巴巴、State Farm 和德国电信等大公司如何分享他们使用 Flux 取得的成功。每天都有新的采用者将自己添加到 Flux 快乐用户列表并分享他们对 Flux 的热爱，最近的一个是 美国国防部。

Flux 的成功现在达到了另一个水平，因为 CTO、GM 和其他人将在 10 月 20 日证明他们如何信任 Flux 来交付 GitOps。 

您知道所有这些产品都使用面向企业的 Flux 吗？

  • Amazon EKS Anywhere
  • D2iQ DKP Kommander
  • Microsoft Azure Arc Kubernetes
  • VMware Tanzu 应用平台
  • Weaveworks Weave GitOps

Brendan Burns（Kubernetes 联合创始人）和 Ihor Dvoretskyi（CNCF 开发者倡导者）也将登台庆祝 CNCF 项目的这一重大事件，该项目也正在迅速完成毕业步骤。

Flux 有哪些新功能可以继续获得这些类型公司的成功、安全性、可靠性和信任？ 

  • API 稳定性：API 自 2021 年 6 月以来一直保持稳定，并持续改进。 美国国防部最近分享了 Flux 成为他们明确选择的众多原因之一。
  • 云支持：该团队增加了对各种云产品的支持，因此您的通用工具应该就位（ssh/gpg 密钥格式、git 子模块、扩展 SOPS 支持）以及对混合架构集群的全面支持。我们对供应商的支持包括将 Flux 添加到 OpenShift OperatorHub，以便用户现在可以通过 Flux 使用 OpenShift 获取 GitOps。 
  • 提高性能：通过使用服务器端应用（自 Kubernetes 1.18 起可用）进行协调，我们能够提高性能（CPU、内存、网络、FD 使用）并减少对 Kubernetes API 的调用次数。 
  • 同时使用 CRD 和 CRer：由于使用服务器端应用进行这项工作，Flux 现在可以验证并协调同时包含 CRD 和 CR 的源。 
  • 改进的可靠性、偏差检测和可观察性： Flux 现在可以利用服务器端应用，更可靠地检测和报告所需状态（git、s3 等）和集群状态之间的偏差。用户现在可以等待所有应用的资源准备就绪，而无需填写运行状况检查列表。通过实时报告垃圾收集和运行状况评估操作，这提高了协调过程的整体可观察性。
  • 工作集成：集成对于用户至关重要，因此 Fluxcd/pkg 现在封装了 GitOps 控制器的最佳实践。 
  • 兼容性：Flux 的首要任务是与您经常使用的所有工具良好配合。现在，我们的通知控制器（微服务设计的 Flux 项目的一个组件）可以在几乎所有主要协议上发送警报和通知。 
  • Flagger：新功能列表已经增加，并且正在基于 Flux 控制器进行重新构建。

因此，Flux 社区的用户、贡献者和维护者不断增长也就不足为奇了。 10 月 20 日，请访问 www.gitopsdays.com，聆听一些最伟大支持者的意见。

item.title: 【Cilium joins CNCF as an incubating project】Cilium 作为孵化项目加入 CNCF
item.description:
【

The CNCF Technical Oversight Committee (TOC) has voted to accept Cilium as a CNCF incubating project. 

Cilium provides networking, security, and observability for cloud native environments by acting as a CNI and enhanced networking layer for Kubernetes using eBPF.

“Cilium and eBPF are changing the networking and security landscape, with the majority of big cloud providers now relying on Cilium,” said Thomas Graf, co-creator of Cilium. “Organizations across financial services, cloud, hyperscalers, and enterprises use Cilium for its deep security, performance, scalability, and observability. The CNCF is the ideal home for Cilium and its entire community as the project evolves further.”

Cilium is used across the Kubernetes ecosystem by organizations like Adobe, Capital One, Cognite, Datadog, GitLab, Palantir, SAP Concur, Telenor, Trip.com, Wildlife Studios, Yahoo, and many more. In addition, cloud providers such as Alibaba, AWS, DigitalOcean, and Google Cloud use Cilium as the CNI plugin of choice for managed cloud and on-premises Kubernetes platforms. Cilium is tightly integrated with Envoy and Prometheus and provides an extension framework based on Go.

“Cilium is a critical part of the Datadog network stack as it provides consistent Kubernetes networking across cloud providers as well as performant and secure communications thanks to eBPF,” said Laurent Bernaille, Staff Engineer at Datadog.

“Cilium brings the power of eBPF to the world of Kubernetes. This enables GKE to offer greater observability, security, flexibility, and efficiency of the datapath to its customers,” said Weilong Cui, Senior Software Engineer, Google.

Main Components:

The Cilium project consists of multiple components and layers which can be used independently of each other. This allows users to pick a particular functionality or to run Cilium in combination with other CNIs.

  • Agent: The agent runs on all Kubernetes worker nodes and other servers hosting workloads. It provides the core eBPF platform and is the foundation for all other Cilium components.
  • Network Plugins (CNI): The CNI plugin enables organizations to use Cilium to provide networking for Kubernetes clusters and other orchestration systems which rely on the CNI specification.
  • Hubble: Hubble is the observability component of Cilium. It provides network and security logs, metrics, tracing data, and several graphical user interfaces. 
  • ClusterMesh: ClusterMesh implements a network or service mesh that can span multiple clusters and external workloads running on external virtual machines or bare-metal servers. It provides connectivity, service discovery, network security, and observability across clusters and workloads. 
  • Load Balancer: The load balancer is capable of running in the cluster to implement Kubernetes services and standalone to provide north-south load-balancing in front of Kubernetes clusters. 

Notable Milestones:

  • > 9K GitHub Stars 
  • > 12K pull requests 
  • 342 GitHub contributors
  • > 365+ Releases
  • > 8.3K Slack members

“eBPF allows programs in the kernel to run without kernel modules or modifications,” said Chris Aniszczyk, CTO of CNCF. “It is enabling a new generation of software to extend the behavior of the kernel. In the case of Cilium, it provides sidecar less high-performance networking, advanced load balancing, and more. We’re excited to welcome more eBPF-based projects into the cloud native ecosystem and look forward to watching Cilium help grow the eBPF ecosystem.”

As a CNCF incubating project, Cilium has planned a full roadmap and is actively adding new features and functionality. The team will be adding new service mesh functionality, including support for the OpenTelemetry project and L7 load balancing controls, building on the existing Envoy Proxy integration. They will also incorporate additional features for on-premises deployments, including advanced IPAM modes, multi-homing, service changing, and enhanced support for external workloads. Finally, the team will focus on evolving security capabilities by adding further identity integrations, deeper workload visibility, and continuing to focus on identity-based enforcement. 

As a CNCF-hosted project, joining incubating technologies Argo, Buildpacks, CloudEvents, CNI, Contour, Cortex, CRI-O, Crossplane, Dragonfly, emissary-ingress, Falco, Flagger, Flux, gRPC, KEDA, KubeEdge, NATS, Notary, OpenTelemetry, Operator Framework, SPIFFE, SPIRE, and Thanos, Cilium is part of a neutral foundation aligned with its technical interests, as well as the larger Linux Foundation, which provides governance, marketing support, and community outreach. For more information on maturity requirements for each level, please visit the CNCF Graduation Criteria.

】

CNCF 技术监督委员会 (TOC) 已投票同意 Cilium 作为 CNCF 孵化项目。 

Cilium 通过充当使用 eBPF 的 Kubernetes 的 CNI 和增强网络层，为云原生环境提供网络、安全性和可观察性。

“Cilium 和 eBPF 正在改变网络和安全格局，大多数大型云提供商现在都依赖 Cilium，”Cilium 联合创始人 Thomas Graf 说道。 “金融服务、云、超大规模企业和企业等组织都利用 Cilium 的深度安全性、性能、可扩展性和可观察性。随着项目的进一步发展，CNCF 是 Cilium 及其整个社区的理想家园。”

Cilium 被 Adobe、Capital One、Cognite、Datadog、GitLab、Palantir、SAP Concur、Telenor、Trip.com、Wildlife Studios、Yahoo 等组织在整个 Kubernetes 生态系统中使用，还有更多。此外，阿里巴巴、AWS、DigitalOcean 和 Google Cloud 等云提供商使用 Cilium 作为托管云和本地 Kubernetes 平台的首选 CNI 插件。 Cilium 与 Envoy 和 Prometheus 紧密集成，并提供基于 Go 的扩展框架。

“Cilium 是 Datadog 网络堆栈的关键部分，因为它可以跨云提供商提供一致的 Kubernetes 网络，并通过 eBPF 提供高性能且安全的通信，”Datadog 的工程师 Laurent Bernaille 说道。

“Cilium 将 eBPF 的强大功能引入 Kubernetes 世界。这使 GKE 能够为其客户提供更高的可观察性、安全性、灵活性和数据路径效率。”Google 高级软件工程师 Weilong Cui 说道。

主要组件：

Cilium 项目由多个组件和层组成，这些组件和层可以相互独立使用。这允许用户选择特定功能或将 Cilium 与其他 CNI 结合运行。

  • 代理：代理在所有 Kubernetes 工作节点和托管工作负载的其他服务器上运行。它提供了核心 eBPF 平台，是所有其他 Cilium 组件的基础。
  • 网络插件 (CNI)：CNI 插件使组织能够使用 Cilium 为 Kubernetes 集群和其他依赖 CNI 规范的编排系统提供网络。
  • Hubble：Hubble 是 Cilium 的可观察性组件。它提供网络和安全日志、指标、跟踪数据和多个图形用户界面。 
  • ClusterMesh：ClusterMesh 实现了一个网络或服务网格，可以跨越多个集群以及在外部虚拟机或裸机服务器上运行的外部工作负载。它提供连接、服务跨集群和工作负载的发现、网络安全和可观察性。 
  • 负载均衡器：负载均衡器既可以运行在集群中实现 Kubernetes 服务，也可以独立运行在 Kubernetes 集群前面提供南北负载均衡。 

值得注意的里程碑：

  • > 9K GitHub Stars
  • > 12K Pull requests
  • 342 GitHub 贡献者
  • > 365+ 版本
  • > 8.3K Slack 成员

“eBPF 允许内核中的程序无需内核模块或修改即可运行，”CNCF 首席技术官 Chris Aniszczyk 说道。 “它使新一代软件能够扩展内核的行为。就 Cilium 而言，它提供了无边车的高性能网络、高级负载平衡等。我们很高兴欢迎更多基于 eBPF 的项目进入云原生生态系统，并期待看到 Cilium 帮助发展 eBPF 生态系统。”

作为 CNCF 孵化项目，Cilium 规划了完整的路线图，并正在积极添加新特性和功能。该团队将在现有 Envoy 代理集成的基础上添加新的服务网格功能，包括对 OpenTelemetry 项目和 L7 负载平衡控制的支持。它们还将整合用于本地部署的附加功能，包括高级 IPAM 模式、多宿主、服务更改以及对外部工作负载的增强支持。最后，该团队将通过添加进一步的身份集成、更深入的工作负载可见性并继续关注基于身份的实施来专注于不断发展的安全功能。 

作为 CNCF 托管的项目，加入孵化技术 Argo、Buildpacks、CloudEvents、CNI、Contour、Cortex、CRI-O、Crossplane、Dragonfly、emissary-ingress、Falco、Flagger、Flux、gRPC、KEDA、KubeEdge、NATS、Notary、OpenTelemetry、Operator Framework、SPIFFE、SPIRE 和 Thanos，Cilium 是与其技术保持一致的中立基金会的一部分利益，以及更大的 Linux 基金会，该基金会提供治理、营销支持和社区外展。有关每个级别的成熟度要求的更多信息，请访问 CNCF 毕业标准。

item.title: 【Diversity in Cloud Native: Results from our first-ever community survey on Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion】云原生的多样性：我们首次关于多样性、公平性和包容性的社区调查结果
item.description:
【

CNCF recently conducted its first microsurvey to better understand the state of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) in our community. 

This informal survey was designed to create a window into community perspectives on DEI, a core tenet of the cloud native community. CNCF and the entire open source ecosystem have seen rapid growth of projects, and more open source software driving more of the world’s innovation. We felt it was an important time to check in with the community to see if there were places we could provide more support in terms of DEI. 

We found that more than 7 of 10 respondents (75%) said open source is becoming more inclusive. This is good news, but the responses also indicate that different groups experience different levels of inclusivity. For instance, men are slightly more likely to feel a strong sense of belonging in the OSS community than women and non-binary individuals, with 77% saying they agree or strongly agree with this sentiment versus 71% of women and 64% non-binary individuals.

Overall, there is room for improvement. More than three-quarters of everyone surveyed believe OSS would be more resilient and innovative with increased diversity of contributors, with men only slightly less convinced than other groups.

  • Some 89% of individuals who do not identify as straight agree with this statement compared with 78% of heterosexuals.
  • Both Caucasians and minority groups were at 80%.
  • 89% of women and 100% of non-binary individuals agreed with this statement compared with 77% of men.

View the full results of the survey here.

Both the Linux Foundation and CNCF expect to conduct regular surveys on the topic to inform us about what is working and what isn’t. We’ll be better positioned to track progress and learn where we need to do more work.

】

CNCF 最近进行了首次微观调查，以更好地了解我们社区的多样性、公平性和包容性 (DEI) 状况。 

这项非正式调查旨在创建一个了解社区对 DEI 观点的窗口，DEI 是云原生社区的核心原则。 CNCF 和整个开源生态系统的项目数量快速增长，更多的开源软件推动了世界更多的创新。我们认为现在是与社区沟通的重要时刻，看看我们是否可以在 DEI 方面提供更多支持。 

我们发现超过十分之七的受访者 (75%) 表示开源正在变得更具包容性。这是个好消息，但调查结果也表明不同群体的包容性程度不同。例如，男性比女性和非二元个体更有可能在 OSS 社区中感受到强烈的归属感，77% 的人表示他们同意或强烈同意这种情绪，而女性和非二元个体的这一比例分别为 71% 和 64%。

总体而言，还有改进的空间。超过四分之三的受访者认为，随着贡献者多样性的增加，OSS 将更具弹性和创新性，而男性的信念仅略低于其他群体。

  • 大约 89% 的非异性恋者同意这一说法，而异性恋者的这一比例为 78%。
  • 白人和少数族裔的比例均为 80%。
  • 89%的女性和 100% 的非二元性别个体同意这一说法，而男性的这一比例为 77%。

在此处查看调查的完整结果。

Linux 基金会和 CNCF 都希望就该主题进行定期调查，以告知我们什么是有效的，什么是无效的。我们将能够更好地跟踪进度并了解需要在哪些方面做更多工作。
